utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: karen nelson is a 3 -year-old female with no significant past medical history who comes in for evaluation of a new right eye twitch karen is accompanied by her father hi karen how are you

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm okay i guess

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey dad how are you doing

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: hey doc i am okay yeah karen has been having this eye twitch i noticed a couple of weeks ago when i talked to her pediatrician and they told me to come see you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so karen have you felt the twitch

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah well i mean i feel my face sometimes

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah and do you have any pain when it happens

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no it it does n't really hurt but i noticed that dad looks real nervous when it happens

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah i i i can understand that's because he loves you do you feel the urge to move your face

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sometimes and then it moves and then i feel better

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay and so so dad how often are you seeing the twitch on karen

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't know i mean it varies sometimes i see it several times an hour and there is other days we do n't see it at all until sometimes late afternoon but we definitely notice it you know everyday for the last several weeks

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so karen how is how is how is soccer

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i like soccer

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah dad dad takes me to play every saturday

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it's it's pretty fun but there's this girl named isabella she she plays rough

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: does she

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: she yeah she tries to kick me and she pulls my hair and

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: oh

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sometimes she's not very nice

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that is n't very nice you gon na have to show her that that's not very nice you're gon na have to teach her a lesson

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah and and then sometimes after soccer we we go and i get mcdugge's and it and it's it makes for a nice day with dad

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: is that your favorite at mcdonald's in the the mcnuggates

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not not really but they are cheap so

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright well you you made dad happy at least right

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah that's what he says because i'm expensive because i want dresses and dogs and stuff all the time

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah well yeah who does n't well okay well hopefully we will get you you know squared away here so you can you know play your soccer and go shopping for dresses with dad so so dad tell me does the karen seem bothered or any other and have any other issues when this happens

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i mean when it happens she just continues playing or doing whatever she was doing when it happens

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright has she has she otherwise been feeling okay since this started has she been acting normally

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i i'd say she seems fine i mean she has been eating well and playing with her friends and she goes about her normal activities really

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay good

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: never even though anything was going on

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright good so has has karen had any seizures in the past

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: no okay and then so tell me when the twitch occurs do you ever notice any you know parts of her like moving or twitching

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well no uh it's just her face

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean the whole side of her face moves when it happens it seems like it several seconds and then it finally stops and she just seems to be blinking frequently and and and you know wait a minute i i did make a video so you can see just in case it does n't do it during the visit

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay yeah that would be great to see that because i wan na see what's going on so thank you for that tell me is there any family history of seizures or like tourette's syndrome

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well no history of seizures but i i i never heard of that tourette thing

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah so so toret is that it's a nervous system disorder that you know involves like repetitive movements or like unwanted sounds and it typically begins in childhood and i do n't know have you noticed anything like that with her when she was younger

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: really i had nobody in our family got anything like that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay now tell me have you noticed any other symptoms how about like fever or chills

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay coughing headache

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: ma'am

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay how about any problems with karen's sleep

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: nope

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay good let's go ahead and do physical exam on karen here alright karen i'm just gon na take a look at you and and ask you to follow some commands okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright can you follow my finger with your eyes good now can you do me a favor walk across the room for me great job okay now i want you to close your eyes and reach out your arms in front of you good now keep your eyes closed can you feel me touch you here how about okay how about there

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: does that feel the same

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright so i'm just gon na check your reflexes okay alright now on your on the neurological exam the patient is awake alert and oriented times three speech is clear and fluent gait is steady heel toe walking is normal and the cranial nerves are intact without focal neurologic findings there is no pronator drift sensation is intact reflexes are two plus and symmetric at the biceps triceps knees and ankles so this means everything looks good karen

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that's great

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good alright so i'm gon na go ahead and tell you what we're gon na do so i'm gon na tell you my assessment and plan here so dad so for the first problem i do believe that karen does have a tick eye tics are very common in children and as many as you know one in five children have a tick during their school years and tics can also include things like shoulder shrugging facial grimacing sniffling excessive throat clearing and uncontrolled vocalization i can say that essentially they're brief sudden and involuntary motor movements now we do n't have a full understanding of the cause of the tics but they typically occur around five to ten years of age but most ticks go away on their own and they disappear within a year so these are what we call transient tics and the best thing to do is ignore the tics so it does n't seem to be bothering karen and she seems to be doing well in school and activities so it may wax and wane over time but you might notice it more towards the end of the day when the child is tired so you may also you know see it if they're stressed so that's why it's important to just ignore it now when you draw attention to the tick it does make the child conscious so that can make the tic worse so we want to be careful again just to to kind of not to draw too much attention on it and do you have any questions for me

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so you mean you're telling me you do n't think he had a seizure or a bit or nothing

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah i do n't think it's i do n't think so because it's it is the same part of her body that's moving every time that and she reports that it's somewhat of an there is an urge to blink her eye and some relief afterwards

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so you're not recommending any kind of treatment there is no pill or cream or nothing

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: not at this time because she seems to be doing well overall and the tic has n't impacted her school or her activities but if it worsens then we can consider some treatment okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: alright alright sounds good

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright thank you you guys have a good day

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: doctor

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: bye karen

detail_list.summary: Karen Nelson is a 3-year-old female with no significant past medical history presenting for a right eye twitch.
Right eye twitch.
The doctor asks the patient if she feels the twitch.
The doctor asks the patient if she experiences pain with the twitch.
The doctor asks the patient if she feels an urge to move her face.
The doctor asks the father about the frequency of the twitch.
The father reports the twitch varies in frequency, occurring several times an hour or sometimes not until late afternoon, but has been present daily for several weeks.
The patient plays soccer on Saturdays and reports a peer is physically aggressive toward her.
The doctor asks if the patient is bothered by the twitch or has other issues.
The father reports the patient continues her normal activities when the twitch occurs.
The doctor asks if the patient has been feeling well and acting normally.
The father reports the patient is eating well and participating in normal activities.
The doctor asks if the patient has a history of seizures.
The doctor asks if the father notices other body parts moving or twitching.
The father describes the twitch as involving the side of the face and frequent blinking, and provides a video of the event.
The doctor asks about family history of seizures or Tourette's syndrome.
The father reports no family history of seizures or Tourette's syndrome.
The doctor asks about fever or chills.
The doctor asks about cough or headache.
The doctor asks about sleep disturbances.
The doctor performs a physical examination.
Neurological exam findings: alert and oriented x3, clear speech, steady gait, normal heel-toe walking, cranial nerves intact, no pronator drift, sensation intact, reflexes 2+ and symmetric.
The doctor diagnoses transient tics, explains the nature of tics, and advises against drawing attention to them.
The father asks if the patient had a seizure.
The doctor explains why the symptoms are consistent with a tic rather than a seizure.
The father asks if any medical treatment is recommended.
The doctor states no treatment is recommended at this time as the patient is doing well.
